name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٥)
```

```english
We said, ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise and eat thereof freely whencesoever you wish, but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ (35)
```

```arabic
فَأَزَلَّهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ ۖ وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٣٦)
```

```english
Then Satan caused them to stumble from it, and he dislodged them from what [state] they were in; and We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ (36)
```

```arabic
فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَـٰتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٣٧)
```

```english
Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (37)
```

```arabic
قُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. (38)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (39)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** "We said, 'O Adam, dwell...'" (وقلنا يا آدم اسكن): While the story of the angels prostrating to Adam is repeated in several places in the Holy Quran, the story of the Garden (الجنة) occurs in only three places:

**First:** Here, in *Surah al-Baqarah*.

**Second:** In *Surah al-A'raf*. Allah, the Exalted, says: **وَيَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ . فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ لِيُبْدِيَ لَهُمَا مَا وُورِيَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْآتِهِمَا وَقَالَ : مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ الْخَالِدِينَ . وَقَاسَمَهُمَا إِنِّي لَكُمَا لَمِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ . فَدَلَّاهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا الشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ وَنَادَاهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَا إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ . قَالَا : رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ . قَالَ : اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ . قَالَ : فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ** — "‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise, and eat thereof whence you wish; but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ Then Satan tempted them, to expose to them their nakedness that had been hidden from them, and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal.’ And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed a well-wisher to you.’ So he brought about their fall by delusion. So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, “Satan is indeed your manifest enemy”?’ They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.’ He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth.’" (7:19-25).

**Third:** In *Surah Ta-Ha*. Allah, the Exalted, says: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا . وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ . فَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ . إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ ، وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَ . فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ الشَّيْطَانُ قَالَ : يَا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ . فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ . ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ . قَالَ : اهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ . وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ . قَالَ : رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا . قَالَ : كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آيَاتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنسَ** — "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him. When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis; he refused. So We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun.’ Then Satan tempted him. He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of eternity and an undying kingdom?’ So they ate of it, and their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray. Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him. He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind.’ He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, though I used to see?’ He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today.’" (20:115-126).

The context of the verses, especially the saying of the Exalted at the beginning of the story: "Indeed, I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth" (إني جاعل في الأرض خليفة), indicates that Adam (peace be upon him) was created specifically to live on the earth and die therein. Allah only settled them in the Garden to test them and so that their nakedness (سوآتهما) would be exposed to them, leading to their descent to the earth. Similarly, the context of His saying in *Surah Ta-Ha*: "So We said, 'O Adam...'" (فقلنا يا آدم), and in *Surah al-A'raf*: "And O Adam, dwell..." (ويا آدم اسكن), weaves the story of the Garden with the story of the angels' prostration as a single, continuous narrative.

In short, he (peace be upon him) was created to inhabit the earth, and the path to settling on the earth was this very course: his being preferred over the angels to establish his vicegerency (الخلافة), then the command to them to prostrate, then the dwelling in the Garden, and the prohibition from approaching the forbidden tree until they ate from it and their nakedness was exposed, causing them to descend to the earth. Thus, the final factor for settling on the earth and choosing the worldly life (الحيوة الدنيا) was the appearance of the nakedness (السوأة)—which refers to the private parts (العورة), as evidenced by His saying: "and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise." This represents animalistic inclination (التمايل الحيواني), which also necessitates nutrition and growth. Therefore, Iblis had no concern other than exposing their nakedness.

Although Allah had fashioned Adam and his wife with an earthly, human fashioning (تسوية أرضية بشرية) before entering them into the Garden, they did not remain long after this fashioning, nor were they given much respite to complete their perception of their nakedness or other necessities and requirements of worldly life in this world before Allah entered them into the Garden. He entered them into the Garden at a time when they had not yet separated or disconnected their perception from the realm of the spirit (عالم الروح) and the angels (الملائكة). The evidence for this is His saying: "to expose to them what had been hidden (ووري) from them," and He did not say "what *was* hidden (كان ووري) from them." This suggests that the concealment of the nakedness was not possible in the worldly life as a permanent state; rather, it was maintained for a brief moment, followed immediately by the dwelling in the Garden.

Thus, the appearance of the nakedness was a decreed and inevitable matter (مقضياً محتوماً) in the earthly life and upon eating from the tree. For this reason, the Exalted said: "So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable (فتشقى)," and He said: "and He expelled them from what they were in." Furthermore, the Exalted forgave their mistake after they repented, yet He did not return them to the Garden; instead, He sent them down to the world to live therein. Had the earthly life, along with the eating from the tree and the appearance of the nakedness, not been an inevitable decree, and had returning to the Garden thereafter not been impossible, they would have returned to it after the removal of the mistake. Thus, the factor in their exit from the Garden and their descent was the eating from the tree and the appearance of the nakedness, which occurred through the whispering (وسوسة) of the accursed Satan.

The Exalted said in *Surah Ta-Ha* at the beginning of the story: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا** — "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him." Then He narrated the story. Is this covenant (العهد) His saying: "do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers"? Or is it His saying: "This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate"? Or is it the covenant in the sense of the universal covenant (الميثاق العمومي) taken from all human beings, and from the prophets specifically in a more emphasized and solemn manner?

The first possibility is incorrect because of His saying: "Then Satan tempted them... and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels...’ and he swore to them..." These verses show that at the time of committing the mistake and approaching the tree, they were mindful of the prohibition. Yet the Exalted said: "but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him." Therefore, the mentioned covenant is not the prohibition against approaching the tree. As for the second possibility—that the mentioned covenant is the warning against following Iblis—while it is not entirely far-fetched, the apparent meaning of the verses does not support it, as the covenant is specified to Adam (peace be upon him) alone, as is evident from the verse.

Furthermore, the warning against Iblis was for both of them together. Also, the conclusion of the verses in *Surah Ta-Ha*, which corresponds to their beginning, suits the covenant in the sense of the universal covenant (الميثاق الكلي), not the warning against Iblis. The Exalted says: **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ** — "Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind."

By way of application, His saying: "But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life" corresponds to the forgetting of the covenant. As you can see, this is more fitting for the covenant in the sense of the covenant of Lordship (الميثاق على الربوبية) and servitude (العبودية) than for the warning against Iblis. Conceptually, there is little correlation between "disregarding the remembrance" and "following Iblis," whereas it is most fitting for the covenant of Lordship. The covenant of Lordship means that man should never forget that the Exalted is his Lord—meaning his Owner (مالكاً) and Governor (مدبراً)—and that man should never, in any state, forget that he is an absolute possession (مملوك طلق) who owns nothing for himself: neither benefit nor harm, neither death nor life nor resurrection; neither in essence (ذاتاً), nor attribute (وصفاً), nor action (فعلاً).

The mistake (الخطيئة) that stands in opposition to this is man's disregard for the remembrance of his Lord's station and his heedlessness of Him by turning toward himself or toward that which pertains to himself of the fleeting and decaying ornaments of the worldly life.

However, if you look closely at the worldly life in its various aspects, its scattered parts, and its commonality between the believer and the disbeliever, you will find it, in reality and in its inner depth (الباطن), to be different in the two cases according to the taste of knowledge of Allah (العلم بالله) and ignorance of Him. The one who knows the station of his Lord (العارف بمقام ربه), when he looks at himself and at the worldly life—which encompasses all types of impurities, varieties of pain, and sorts of hardships such as death and life, health and sickness, abundance and poverty, ease and toil, gain and loss—sees that all of it (whether within the human soul or outside it) is owned by his Lord. Nothing has independence in or of itself; rather, everything belongs to Him who possesses only beauty (الحسن), splendor (البهاء), and goodness (الخير) as befits His might and majesty. Nothing emanates from His presence except the beautiful and the good.

When he looks at things in this way, he sees no hardship that he dislikes, no fear that he dreads, no awe that he shudders at, and no danger that he guards against. Rather, he sees everything he beholds as beautiful and beloved, except what his Lord commands him to dislike or hate; even then, he hates it because of His command, and he loves what He loves and finds pleasure and joy in His command. He has no concern other than his Lord. All this is because he sees everything as the absolute property of his Lord, with no other having any share or portion in it. What business has he with the Owner of the affair and how He disposes of His property—whether by giving life or death, benefit or harm, and so on? This is the "wholesome life" (الحيوة الطيبة) in which there is no misery whatsoever; it is a light with no darkness, a joy with no grief, a finding with no loss, and a wealth with no poverty—all through Allah, the Glorified.

In contrast to this life is the life of the one ignorant of his Lord's station. This poor soul, due to his disconnection from his Lord, does not cast his gaze upon any existent—himself or otherwise—without seeing it as independent in itself, harmful or beneficial, good or evil. Thus, he fluctuates in his life between fear of what he dreads losing, caution against what he fears happening, grief over what escapes him, and regret over what he loses of status, wealth, children, supporters, or anything else he loves, relies upon, and prefers.

Whenever his skin is toughened by habituating to hardship and settling into bitterness, it is replaced by another skin so that he may taste the punishment with a heart that is agitated and anxious, an inner being that is melting and burning, and a chest that is narrow and constrained as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah place abomination upon those who do not believe.

Once you understand this, you will know that the return of both matters—namely, the forgetting of the covenant and the misery of the worldly life—is one, and that worldly misery is a branch of forgetting the covenant.

This is what His speech, the Glorified, suggests when He presented the comprehensive obligation for the people of the world in *Surah Ta-Ha*, saying: **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ . وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ** — "Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind."

And He replaced this in this Surah with His saying: **فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** — "then whoever follows My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve."

From here, you may surmise, if you are discerning, that the tree was a tree whose approach entailed the toil and misery of the worldly life—which is for man to live in the world forgetting his Lord and heedless of His station. It is as if Adam (peace be upon him) wanted to combine the tree with the covenant taken from him, but he was unable to do so; thus, he forgot the covenant and fell into the toil of the worldly life, which was then remedied for him through repentance (التوبة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** "and eat thereof in abundance (رغداً)" (وكلا منها رغداً): *Al-Raghad* (الرغد) means pleasantness and goodness of life. It is said, "the people made their livestock *arghada*" when they left them to graze as they pleased. "A *raghad* people" and " *raghad* women" mean those possessing a prosperous life.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** "but do not approach this tree" (ولا تقربا هذه الشجرة): It seems the prohibition was actually against eating the fruit, but it was attached to "approaching" the tree to signal the severity of the prohibition and as an exaggeration in emphasis. This is evidenced by His saying: "So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them" (*al-A'raf*: 22), and His saying: "So they ate of it, and their nakedness became exposed to them" (*Ta-Ha*: 121). Thus, the violation was by eating, which is what was forbidden by the words "do not approach."

**His saying, the Exalted:** "lest you should be among the wrongdoers (الظالمين)" (فتكونا من الظالمين): This is from *dhulm* (ظلم) — *wrongdoing or injustice*, not from *dhulmah* (ظلمة) — *darkness*, as some have supposed. They both admitted to their wrongdoing when they said, as Allah narrated from them: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves (ظلمنا أنفسنا)! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us..."

However, in *Surah Ta-Ha*, Allah replaced this phrase—namely, "lest you should be among the wrongdoers"—with His saying: "or you will be miserable (فتشقى)." Misery (الشقاء) here means toil (التعب). Then He explained and detailed this toil, saying: **إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ** — "Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun."

From this, it becomes clear that the consequence of this wrongdoing was falling into the toil of the life of this world—hunger, thirst, nakedness, and hardship. Accordingly, the wrongdoing on their part was only wrongdoing against themselves, not in the sense of technical sin (المعصية المصطلحة) or wrongdoing against Allah, the Glorified. From here, it also appears that this prohibition—namely, "do not approach"—was only a cautionary and advisory prohibition (نهياً تنزيهياً إرشادياً) by which He guided them to what contained the well-being and benefit of the subject in the station of counsel, not a legislative command (نهياً مولوياً).

They only wronged themselves by leaving the Garden. Furthermore, the penalty for violating a legislative command (النهي المولوي) is removed by repentance when it is accepted, but it was not removed in their case. They repented and their repentance was accepted, yet they did not return to the Garden where they had been. Had the obligation not been advisory (إرشادي)—having only ontological consequences (تبعة تكوينية) rather than legislative ones (تشريعية)—the acceptance of repentance would have necessitated their return to the station of proximity (مقام القرب) they were in. More will be said on this later, God willing.

**His saying, the Glorified:** "Then Satan caused them to slip (فأزلهما الشيطان)": The apparent meaning of this sentence, like its counterparts, does not necessarily imply more than Satan's whispering (وسوسة) to them, just as he whispers to us (the children of Adam) by casting suggestions into the heart without the person being seen.

However, the apparent meaning of verses such as His saying in *Surah Ta-Ha*: "So We said, 'O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate,'" indicates that the Exalted showed them Satan and made him known to them by person and eye, not just by description. Likewise, His saying narrating from Satan: "O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of eternity..." where the speech is presented in the form of a narrated address, indicates a perceived speaker.

Similarly, His saying in *Surah al-A'raf*: "And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed a well-wisher to you’" (وقاسمهما إني لكما لمن الناصحين); an oath only comes from a perceived swearer.

Also, His saying: "Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, “Satan is indeed your manifest enemy”?’" All of this indicates that he was appearing to them and they were witnessing him. If their state (peace be upon them) were like ours—not witnessing him during the whispering—it would have been permissible for them to say: "Our Lord, we did not perceive him; we thought these whispers were our own thoughts without sensing his presence or intending to violate Your instruction to beware of his whispering."

In short, they were witnessing him and knew him. The prophets, who are protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, likewise know him and witness him when he confronts them, as mentioned in narrations regarding Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, John, Job, Ishmael, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم).

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of these verses, like His saying: "Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree," suggests that they were with him—may he be cursed—near the tree in the Garden. He had entered the Garden, accompanied them, and deceived them with his whispering. There is no problem in this, as the Garden was not the Garden of Eternity (جنة الخلد) such that Satan could not enter it. The proof for this is the exit of all of them from this Garden.

As for His saying addressing Iblis: "‘Get down from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein. So get out!’" (*al-A'raf*: 13), it is possible that the intended meaning is the exit from the company of the angels, or the exit from the heaven (السماء) insofar as it is a station of proximity and honor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "He said, 'Get down, being enemies of one another...'" (قال اهبطوا بعضكم لبعض عدو): The apparent context is that it is an address to Adam, his wife, and Iblis. Iblis alone was specified with the address in *Surah al-A'raf* where He said: "He said, ‘Get down from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein...’" Thus, His saying "Get down (plural)" (اهبطوا) is like a combination of the two addresses and a narration of a decree by which Allah ordained enmity between the accursed Iblis and Adam, his wife, and their progeny. Likewise, He decreed their life on the earth, their death therein, and their resurrection from it.

The progeny of Adam are included with Adam in this ruling, as may be sensed from the apparent meaning of His saying: "In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth," and as will come in His saying: "Certainly We created you, then We fashioned you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’" (*al-A'raf*: 11).

The angels' prostration to Adam (peace be upon him) was because he was an earthly vicegerent (خليفة أرضي). Thus, the one prostrated to was Adam (peace be upon him), but the ruling of the prostration applied to all of humanity. Adam's standing in the position of the one prostrated to was in the capacity of a prototype (الأنموذج) and a representative (النائب).

In short, this story narrated by Allah—of Adam and his wife dwelling in the Garden, then their descent due to eating from the tree—resembles a parable (المثل) illustrating the state of happiness and dignity man was in before his descent to the world: dwelling in the enclosure of holiness (حظيرة القدس), the abode of loftiness and proximity, the house of blessing, joy, intimacy, and light, with pure companions, spiritual friends, and the neighborhood of the Lord of the worlds.

Then, he chooses in its place every toil, hardship, dislike, and pain by inclining toward a fleeting life and a lowly, putrid carcass. Then, if he returns to his Lord after that, He would return him to the abode of His dignity and happiness. But if he does not return to Him, clings to the earth, and follows his desires, then he has exchanged the blessing of Allah for disbelief and has brought himself to the abode of ruin—Hell, which he shall enter, and an evil settlement it is.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "Then Adam received (فتلقى) certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently" (فتلقى آدم من ربه كلمات فتاب عليه): *Al-talaqqi* (التلقي) is *al-talaqqun* (التلقن) — *the receiving of speech with understanding and comprehension*. This receiving was the path that facilitated Adam's (peace be upon him) repentance.

From this, it appears that repentance (التوبة) is of two types: repentance from Allah, which is His returning to the servant with mercy; and repentance from the servant, which is his returning to Allah with seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) and uprooting the sin.

The servant's repentance is flanked by two repentances from Allah. For the servant is never independent of his Lord in any state; his turning away from sin toward Him requires His success-granting (توفيق), assistance, and mercy so that the repentance may be realized. Then, there is a need for His acceptance, care, and mercy. Thus, when the servant's repentance is accepted, it lies between two repentances from Allah, as indicated by His saying: "Then He turned to them clemently (تاب عليهم) so that they might repent (ليتوبوا)" (*al-Tawbah*: 118).

The reading with *Adam* in the accusative case (نصب) and *words* in the nominative case (رفع) suits this point, although the other reading (with *Adam* in the nominative and *words* in the accusative) does not contradict it either.

As for what these words were, it is possible they are what Allah narrates from them in *Surah al-A'raf*: "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers’" (*al-A'raf*: 23). However, the occurrence of these words—namely, "They said, 'Our Lord, we have wronged...'"—before His saying "He said, 'Get down'" in *Surah al-A'raf*, and the occurrence of His saying "Then Adam received..." after His saying "We said, 'Get down'" in this Surah, does not support this.

**But there is something here:** You have seen at the beginning of the story that when Allah said: "Indeed, I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth," the angels said: "Will You set in it one who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your holiness?" The Exalted did not refute their claim regarding the earthly vicegerent or the accusations they cast; He only answered by teaching Adam all the names (الأسماء).

Had there not been something in the teaching of the names that closed the door to their objection, their speech would not have been cut off, nor would the proof have been established against them. Thus, among the names Allah taught Adam was something that benefits the sinner when he sins and the wrongdoer when he errs. Perhaps his receiving from his Lord pertained to some of those names; so understand this.

Know that although Adam (peace be upon him) wronged himself by casting it onto the brink of destruction and the fork between the paths of happiness and misery—namely, the world—had he remained at his point of descent, he would have perished. And had he returned to his initial happiness, he would have toiled and wronged himself. Thus, he (peace be upon him) was a wrongdoer to himself in every estimation. However, through his descent, he prepared for himself a degree of happiness and a station of perfection that he would not have attained had he not descended, nor would he have attained it had he descended without the mistake.

When could he have witnessed his own poverty, humiliation, destitution, need, and deficiency while he possessed, in every toil and hardship that befell him, a spirit and ease in the enclosure of holiness and the neighborhood of the Lord of the worlds? For Allah has attributes of pardon, forgiveness, repentance, concealment, grace, kindness, and mercy that are only attained by sinners; and He has, in the days of time, fragrances that are only enjoyed by those who expose themselves to them.

This repentance is what necessitated the legislation of the path expected to be traversed and the cleansing of the dwelling hoped to be inhabited; behind it lies the legislation of the religion (الدين) and the establishment of the creed (الملة).

This is evidenced by what you see in Allah's speech, where He repeatedly places repentance before faith (الإيمان). The Exalted said: "So be steadfast as you have been commanded, and those who have repented with you" (*Hud*: 112), and He said: "Indeed I am All-forgiving toward him who repents and believes" (*Ta-Ha*: 82), among other verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "We said, 'Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me...'" (قلنا اهبطوا منها جميعاً فإما يأتينكم مني هدى). This is the first legislation of religion for Adam (peace be upon him) and his progeny. He summarized the entire religion in two sentences to which nothing shall be added until the Day of Resurrection.

If you reflect on this story (the story of the Garden), especially what occurred in *Surah Ta-Ha*, you will find that the course of the story necessitated two decrees (قضائين) from the Exalted regarding Adam and his progeny. The eating from the tree necessitated His ruling and decree of descent, settlement on the earth, and living therein—that miserable life they were warned of when forbidden from approaching the tree.

Secondly, the repentance was followed by a second decree and ruling from the Exalted: honoring Adam and his progeny with guidance (الهداية) toward servitude (العبودية). Thus, what was decreed first was the earthly life itself; then, through repentance, Allah made that life wholesome by mounting upon it the guidance toward servitude. Thus, life was composed of an earthly life and a heavenly life.

This is what is understood from the repetition of the command to descend in this Surah, where the Exalted said: "And We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" and then said: "We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me...’"

The placement of repentance between the two commands to descend suggests that the repentance occurred while they had not yet separated from the Garden, even if they were no longer in it as they had been settled before.

This is also suggested by His saying: "Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from *that* (تلكما) tree...’" after He had said to them: "do not approach *this* (هذه) tree." He used the word "that" (تلكما), which is a demonstrative for the distant, after having used "this" (هذه), which is for the near. And He used the word "called out" (نادى), which is for the distant, after having used "said" (قال), which is for the near; so understand.

Know that the apparent meaning of His saying: "And We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" and His saying: "He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth’" is that the mode of this life after the descent differs from its mode in the Garden before the descent. This is a life whose reality is intertwined with the reality of the earth, characterized by toil and misery, necessitating that man be formed in the earth, then returned to it by death, then brought out from it by resurrection.

Thus, the earthly life differs from the life of the Garden; the life of the Garden was a heavenly life, not an earthly one.

From here, one can conclude with certainty that Adam's Garden was in the heaven (السماء), even if it was not the Garden of the Hereafter—the Garden of Eternity from which no one who enters ever exits.

Yes, the discussion remains regarding the meaning of "the heaven" (السماء), and perhaps we shall be granted the success to exhaustively research it, God willing.

One thing remains here, which is the discussion regarding Adam's mistake (خطيئة). We say: although the apparent meaning of the verses at first glance suggests the occurrence of disobedience (المعصية) and mistake from him (peace be upon him)—as the Exalted said: "lest you should be among the wrongdoers," and "Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray," and as he admitted in what Allah narrated from them: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers"—

However, reflection on the verses of the story and precision regarding the prohibition issued against eating from the tree necessitates the certainty that the mentioned prohibition was not a legislative prohibition (نهياً مولوياً). Rather, it was an advisory prohibition (نهي إرشادي) intended to guide and direct toward the benefit and good inherent in the subject of the obligation, not a legislative command or will.

This is evidenced by: **First:** The Exalted made "wrongdoing" (الظلم) a consequence of the prohibition in this Surah and in *Surah al-A'raf*, saying: "do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers." Then He replaced it in *Surah Ta-Ha* with His saying: "or you will be miserable (فتشقى)," making it a consequence of leaving the Garden. Misery (الشقاء) means toil (التعب). Then He mentioned after it, as if in explanation: "Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun."

Thus, He clarified that the intended misery is worldly toil, which this earthly life entails—hunger, thirst, nakedness, and so on.

Guarding against these matters is what necessitated such a prohibition, not any other legislative aspect. Thus, the prohibition was advisory, and violating an advisory prohibition does not constitute a legislative disobedience or a transgression of the bounds of servitude. Accordingly, the intended "wrongdoing" (الظلم) in the verses is their wrongdoing against themselves by casting themselves into toil and destruction, not the blameworthy wrongdoing in the context of Lordship and servitude; this is clear.

**Second:** Repentance (التوبة)—which is the return from the servant—when followed by acceptance from the side of the Master, necessitates that the sin becomes as if it were no sin, and the disobedience as if it never occurred. The repentant sinner is treated as an obedient and submissive one, and his action is treated as compliance and submission.

Had the prohibition against eating from the tree been legislative, and had the repentance been for a servant's sin and a return from violating a legislative prohibition, it would have been necessary for them to return to the Garden; yet they did not return.

From here, it is known that the consequence of the forbidden eating—namely, the exit from the Garden—was a necessary, ontological consequence (إستتباعاً ضرورياً تكوينياً), similar to the consequence of poison leading to death or fire leading to burning, as is the case in advisory obligations. It was not a consequence of the nature of legislative punishment (المجازاة المولوية) in legislative obligations—such as entering the Fire for one who abandons prayer, or deserving blame and necessitating distance in general, social, legislative violations.

**Third:** His saying, the Exalted: "We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it forever.’"

This is a comprehensive statement for all detailed legislations that Allah sent down in this world through His angels, books, and messengers. It narrates the first legislation enacted for man in this world—which is the world of Adam and his progeny. As Allah narrates, it occurred after the second command to descend. It is clear that the command to descend is an ontological command (أمر تكويني) subsequent to being in the Garden and committing the mistake. Thus, at the time of violating the prohibition and approaching the tree, there was neither a legislated religion nor a legislative obligation. Therefore, no servant's sin or legislative disobedience was realized at that time.

This does not contradict the fact that the address to the angels and Iblis to prostrate—which was before the address "do not approach"—was a legislative address, because the subject of the obligation (المكلف) was different.

If you say: "If the prohibition was advisory and not legislative, what is the meaning of Allah counting their action as wrongdoing (ظلم), disobedience (عصيان), and straying (غواية)?"

I say: As for wrongdoing, it has already been mentioned that the intended meaning is their wrongdoing against themselves in the presence of Allah. As for disobedience (العصيان), linguistically it means the lack of being affected (عدم الإنفعال) or being affected with difficulty, as it is said: "I broke it and it broke (إنكسر), and I broke it and it disobeyed (عصى)." Disobedience—which is the lack of being affected by a command or prohibition—occurs in the context of advisory addresses just as it occurs in legislative obligations.

As for the meaning of disobedience (المعصية) becoming restricted in these times among us Muslims to the violation of commands like "pray," "fast," "perform Hajj," or "do not drink wine," "do not commit adultery," and the like—this is a restriction by way of technical religious terminology (الحقيقة الشرعية أو المتشرعة) which does not harm the generality of the meaning according to language and general usage.

As for straying (الغواية), it is man's inability, for example, to maintain the objective and manage himself in his livelihood in a way that suits and accords with the objective.

Clearly, this varies according to the context, whether advisory or legislative.

If you say: "Then what is the meaning of repentance (التوبة) and their saying: ‘If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers’?"

I say: Repentance, as mentioned, is "returning," and returning varies according to its contexts.

Just as it is permissible for a servant who rebels against his master's command and will to repent to him, so that his lost station of proximity to him may be restored; it is likewise permissible for a sick person—whom a doctor has forbidden, through an advisory prohibition, from eating a specific fruit or food solely for the sake of his health and well-being—to not desist from the prohibition, commit it, suffer harm, and be on the brink of destruction.

It is permissible for him to "repent" to the doctor so that he may point him toward a medicine that restores him to his previous state and health. The doctor would then mention to him that this requires enduring toil, hardship, effort, and discipline for a period until he returns to his initial healthy temperament, or even to one more noble and better than it.

As for forgiveness (المغفرة), mercy (الرحمة), and loss (الخسران), the discussion regarding them is similar to the discussion regarding their counterparts in their variation according to their contexts.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, narrated from his father in a *marfu'* report, he said: Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about Adam's Garden: "Was it one of the gardens of the world or one of the gardens of the Hereafter?" He (peace be upon him) replied: **كانت من جنان الدنيا تطلع فيها الشمس والقمر ، ولو كانت من جنان الآخرة ما خرج منها أبداً** — "It was one of the gardens of the world, in which the sun and the moon rose. Had it been one of the gardens of the Hereafter, he would never have exited from it." He (peace be upon him) said: "When Allah settled him in the Garden and made it permissible for him except for the tree—because He created him with a constitution that only subsists through command, prohibition, food, clothing, shelter, and marriage, and he does not perceive what benefits him from what harms him except through success-granting (توفيق)—Iblis came to him and said: 'If both of you eat from this tree which Allah forbade you, you will become two angels and remain in the Garden forever; and if you do not eat from it, Allah will expel you from the Garden.' And he swore to them that he was a well-wisher to them, as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said narrating from him: **ما نهيكما ربكما عن هذه الشجرة الا أن تكونا ملكين أو تكونا من الخالدين وقاسمهما اني لكما لمن الناصحين** — 'Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal. And he swore to them, “I am indeed a well-wisher to you.”' So Adam accepted his word and they both ate from the tree."

"So they became as Allah narrated: their nakedness became exposed to them, and what Allah had clothed them with of the Garden fell from them. They began to cover themselves with the leaves of the Garden. Their Lord called out to them: 'Did I not forbid you from that tree and tell you that Satan is a manifest enemy to you?' They said, as Allah narrated from them: 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.' Then Allah said to them: 'Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.' He said: 'Meaning until the Day of Resurrection.'" He said: "So Adam descended upon *al-Safa*—and it was named *al-Safa* because the Chosen One (صفي الله) of Allah descended upon it—and Eve descended upon *al-Marwah*—and it was named *al-Marwah* because the woman (المرئة) descended upon it. Adam remained for forty mornings prostrating and weeping over the Garden. Then Gabriel descended to him and said: 'Did Allah not create you with His hand, breathe into you of His spirit, and make His angels prostrate to you?' He said: 'Yes.' 'And He commanded you not to eat from the tree, yet you disobeyed Him?' Adam said: 'Indeed, Iblis swore to me by Allah falsely.'"

**I say:** There are other narrations from the path of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) stating that Adam's Garden was one of the gardens of the world, although some of them share the same chain of narration as this one through Ibrahim ibn Hashim.

What is meant by it being among the gardens of the world (جنان الدنيا) is that it was a realm of the barzakh (برزخية) — *the intermediate state between death and resurrection* — as opposed to the Gardens of Eternity (جنان الخلد). This is indicated by several passages in the narrations, such as the saying: "So Adam descended upon al-Safa," and the saying: "And Eve descended upon al-Marwah," as well as the saying: "Indeed, what is meant by 'a time' (حين) is the Day of Resurrection." Thus, the stay in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) after death is considered a stay in the earth, in accordance with what appears in the verses of the Resurrection in the Qur’an, which count the stay in the *barzakh* as a stay in the earth. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ * قَالُوا لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَاسْأَلِ الْعَادِّينَ * قَالَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا لَّوْ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "He will say, ‘How many years did you tarry on earth?’ They will say, ‘We tarried a day or part of a day; as those who keep count.’ He will say, ‘You tarried only a little, if you only knew.’" (23:112-114), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ مَا لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ سَاعَةٍ كَذَٰلِكَ كَانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ * وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهَٰذَا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَلَٰكِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** — "On the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear they did not tarry but for an hour: so were they used to being perverted. But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have tarried in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know.’" (30:55-56). Furthermore, a number of narrations transmitted from the People of the House (أهل البيت) indicate that the Garden was in the heaven (السماء) and that they both descended from the heaven. Indeed, one who is familiar with the language of the narrations will not find it strange that the aforementioned Garden was in the heaven, and that the descent from it to the earth occurred despite their having been created in the earth and living therein. This is similar to what has been narrated regarding the Garden being in the heaven, the occurrence of the questioning in the grave therein, and the grave being a meadow from the meadows of Paradise or a pit from the pits of the Fire, and so forth. I hope that this difficulty and similar ones will be resolved in the upcoming discussion regarding the heaven (السماء), if Allah the Mighty wills.

As for how Iblis came to them and the means he employed, the authentic (الصحاح) and reliable (المعتبرة) narrations are devoid of any explanation for it.

In some reports, the serpent and the peacock are mentioned as helpers to Iblis in his seduction of them, but these are not reliable; we have refrained from mentioning them as they seem to be among the extraneous reports (الأخبار الدخيلة). The story is taken from the Torah, and here is the exact wording of the Torah regarding the story:

It says in the second chapter of the first book, which is the Book of Genesis: "And God formed Adam of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and Adam became a living soul. And God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there He put the Adam whom He had formed. And out of the ground made God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil. And a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads. The name of the first is Pishon [the Nile]: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold; and the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone. And the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia. And the name of the third river is Hiddekel [Tigris]: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates. And God took Adam, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it. And God commanded Adam, saying, 'Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat: but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die.' And God said, 'It is not good that Adam should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him.' And out of the ground God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof until now. And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him. And God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, so that he did not feel, and he slept: and He took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof; and the rib, which God had taken from man, made He a woman, and brought her unto Adam. And Adam said, 'This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of man.' Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh. And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed."

**Chapter Three:** "Now the serpent was more subtle than any beast of the field which God had made. And he said unto the woman, 'Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?' And the woman said unto the serpent, 'We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden: but of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die.' And the serpent said unto the woman, 'Ye shall not surely die: for God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods [angels], knowing good and evil with increase.' And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat. And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons. And they heard the voice of God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the voice of God amongst the trees of the garden. And God called unto Adam, and said unto him, 'Where art thou?' And he said, 'I heard Thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself.' And He said, 'Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?' And the man said, 'The woman whom Thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.' And God said unto the woman, 'What is this that thou hast done?' And the woman said, 'The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.' And God said unto the serpent, 'Because thou hast done this with knowledge, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life: and I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.' Unto the woman He said, 'I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee.' And unto Adam He said, 'Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life; thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field; in the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.' And Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living [speaking]. Unto Adam also and to his wife did God make coats of skins, and clothed them. And God said, 'Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:' therefore God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken. So He drove out the man; and He placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life." End of the chapter from (the Arabic Torah printed in the year 1811 CE). By comparing the story from both paths—meaning the path of the Qur’an and the path of the Torah—and then reflecting on the narrations transmitted from both the Sunni (العامة) and Shi'i (الخاصة) sources, you will encounter the realities of the matter. However, we have refrained from diving deep into their explanation and investigation because this book is not intended for that purpose.

As for Iblis entering the Garden and his seduction therein, while it is (firstly) a station of proximity (القرب), sanctity (النزاهة), and purity (الطهارة), and the Exalted has said: **لَّا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ** — "Therein they will not hear any vain talk nor any sinful speech" (52:23); and it is (secondly) in the heaven, and the Exalted said, addressing Iblis when he refused to prostrate to Adam: **فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ** — "‘Go out of it,’ He said, ‘for you are indeed an outcast’" (15:34), and the Exalted said: **فَاهْبِطْ مِنْهَا فَمَا يَكُونُ لَكَ أَن تَتَكَبَّرَ فِيهَا** — "He said, ‘Get down from it! It is not for you to be arrogant here.’" (7:13).

The answer to the first point is as it is sometimes said: the Qur’an only negates the occurrence of vain talk (اللغو) and sin (تأثيم) in the Garden regarding the Garden of Eternity (جنة الخلد) which the believers enter in the Hereafter, and the Garden of the *barzakh* which they enter after death and departure from the realm of obligation (دار التكليف). As for the Garden into which Adam and his wife were admitted, which was before the establishment of man in the realm of obligation and the direction of command and prohibition toward him, the Qur’an has not spoken of any such thing regarding it. Rather, the matter is the opposite, and it suffices you to consider what has been mentioned regarding the occurrence of Adam's disobedience therein. Furthermore, vain talk and sin are relative matters that are only realized after man enters the world, and command and prohibition are directed toward him, and he becomes clothed with obligation (التكليف).

The answer to the second point is, firstly: that the return of the pronoun in His saying: "Go out of it" (فأخرج منها) and His saying: "Get down from it" (فاهبط منها) to the heaven is not apparent from the verse, as the heaven was not mentioned previously in the discourse nor was it already understood. Thus, it is possible that what is meant is the exit from the angels and the descent from them in some sense, or the exit and descent from rank and honor.

**Secondly:** It is possible that the command to descend and exit was a metaphor for the prohibition of staying there among the angels, not for the essence of being there by ascending and passing through without staying or settling like the angels. This is alluded to, or rather witnessed by, what sometimes appears from the verses regarding the eavesdropping (إستراق السمع). It has been narrated that the devils used to ascend before Jesus to the seventh heaven; when Jesus was born, they were barred from the fourth heaven and above; then when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was born, they were barred from all the heavens and were snatched away by the snatching (الخطفة).

**Thirdly:** His speech, the Exalted, is devoid of any mention of Iblis entering the Garden, so there is no occasion for the objection. What has been reported regarding the entry is only in the narrations, and they are solitary reports (آحاد) — *narrations that do not reach the level of mass-concurrence* — not mass-concurrent (متواترة), with the possibility of the narrator having transmitted the meaning rather than the exact words.

The furthest that His speech, the Exalted, indicates regarding his entry into the Garden is His saying, the Exalted, recounting the words of Iblis: **وَقَالَ مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ الْخَالِدِينَ** — "And he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal.’" (7:20), where he used the word "this" (هذه), which is for pointing to something near. However, if it indicated spatial proximity here, it would indicate the same in the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers." (7:19), regarding the Exalted Himself.

In *al-Uyun*, narrated from Abd al-Salam al-Harawi, who said: I said to al-Rida (peace be upon him): "O son of the Messenger of Allah, inform me about the tree from which Adam and Eve ate, what was it? For people have differed regarding it; some narrate that it was wheat, and some narrate that it was the tree of envy (الحسد)." He said: "All of that is true." I said: "Then what is the meaning of these views despite their difference?" He said: "O son of al-Salt, indeed the tree of the Garden bears various kinds; it was a tree of wheat and it had grapes, and it was not like the trees of the world. When Allah, the Exalted, honored Adam by making His angels prostrate to him and by admitting him into the Garden, Adam said: 'Has Allah created any human better than me?' So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knew what had occurred in his soul and called out to him: 'Raise your head, O Adam, and look at the pillar of the Throne.' He looked at the pillar of the Throne and found written upon it: 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, Ali ibn Abi Talib is the Commander of the Faithful, and his wife Fatimah is the Mistress of the Women of the Worlds, and al-Hasan and al-Husayn are the Masters of the Youth of Paradise.' Adam said: 'O Lord, who are these?' He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'O Adam, these are your progeny, and they are better than you and all My creation. Were it not for them, I would not have created you, nor the Garden, nor the Fire, nor the heaven, nor the earth. So beware of looking at them with the eye of envy, lest I remove you from My proximity.' So he looked at them with the eye of envy and desired their station, so the Devil gained mastery over him until he ate from the tree he was forbidden from. And he gained mastery over Eve, so she looked at Fatimah with the eye of envy until she ate from the tree as Adam had eaten. So Allah, the Exalted, removed them from His Garden and sent them down from His proximity to the earth."

**I say:** This meaning has been reported in several narrations, some more elaborate and lengthy than this one, and some more concise and brief.

This narration, as you see, is one in which he (peace be upon him) accepted that the tree was both the tree of wheat and the tree of envy, and that they ate the fruit of the wheat tree and envied and desired the station of Muhammad and his family (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The implication of the first meaning is that the tree was of a lower status than what the people of the Garden would incline toward or desire, while the implication of the second is that it was of a higher status than what Adam and his wife could attain, as in another narration stating it was the tree of the knowledge of Muhammad and his family.

In short, they have two different meanings. However, by referring back to what has passed regarding the matter of the Covenant (الميثاق), you will know that the meaning is one: that Adam (peace be upon him) wanted to combine the enjoyment of the Garden—which is the station of proximity to Allah wherein the Covenant is not to turn to other than Him, the Exalted—and the forbidden tree, which involves the toil of attachment to the world. He was unable to combine the two, so he descended to the earth and forgot the Covenant, and the two matters did not come together for him—which is the station of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then Allah guided him through election (الإجتباء) and detached him from the world through repentance (التوبة), and joined him to what he had forgotten of the Covenant; so understand.

His saying (peace be upon him): "So he looked at them with the eye of envy and desired their station," contains an explanation that what is meant by envy (الحسد) is the desire for their station, rather than the envy that is one of the base character traits.

With the preceding explanation, the apparent contradiction is resolved between what was narrated in *Kamal al-Din* from al-Thumali from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made a covenant with Adam not to approach the tree. When the time came that was in the knowledge of Allah for him to eat from it, he forgot and ate from it, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'And certainly We made a covenant with Adam before, but he forgot, and We did not find in him any resolve,'" the *hadith*.

And between what al-Ayyashi narrated in his *tafsir* from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) when he was asked: "How did Allah take Adam to task for forgetting?" He said: "He did not forget; and how could he forget when he remembers and Iblis says to him: 'Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal'?" the *hadith*. The reason for this is clear.

In *Amali al-Saduq*, narrated from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi, who said: When al-Ma'mun gathered for Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (peace be upon him) the proponents of various doctrines from the people of Islam and the religions of the Jews, Christians, Zoroastrians, Sabaeans, and other proponents of doctrines, no one stood up except that he established his proof against them as if he had silenced them with a stone. Then Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm stood up and said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, do you maintain the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Then what do you do with the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ** (And Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray)?" ... until he said: Our master al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: "Woe to you, O Ali! Fear Allah and do not attribute enormities to the prophets of Allah, and do not interpret the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, according to your own opinion, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ** (and no one knows its interpretation except Allah and those firmly grounded in knowledge). As for His saying, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding Adam: **وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ**, indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created Adam as a Proof (حجة) in His earth and a vicegerent (خليفة) in His lands; He did not create him for the Garden. The disobedience of Adam was in the Garden, not on the earth, so that the decrees of Allah's command might be fulfilled. When he was sent down to the earth and made a Proof and a vicegerent, he was protected (عصم) by His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرَانَ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** (Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the worlds)," the *hadith*.

**I say:** His saying: "And the disobedience was in the Garden, etc." is an allusion to what we previously established: that the religious, masterly obligation (التكليف الدينى المولوي) had not yet been established in the Garden; rather, its locus is the earthly life decreed for Adam (peace be upon him) after the descent to the earth. Thus, the disobedience was only a disobedience to an advisory command (أمر إرشادي), not a masterly one, so there is no reason for the strained interpretations of the *hadith* that some have undertaken.

In *al-Uyun*, narrated from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: I was present at the assembly of al-Ma'mun, and with him was Ali ibn Musa. Al-Ma'mun said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Then what is the meaning of the saying of Allah, the Exalted: 'And Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray'?" He said: "Indeed, Allah, the Exalted, said to Adam: 'Dwell, you and your wife, in the Garden, and eat therefrom freely wherever you wish, but do not approach this tree,' and He pointed for them to the tree of wheat, 'lest you should be among the wrongdoers.' He did not say to them: 'Do not eat from this tree, nor from anything of its kind.' So they did not approach that specific tree and did not eat from it; they only ate from another when the Devil whispered to them and said: 'Your Lord did not forbid you from this tree; He only forbade you from approaching another, and He did not forbid you from eating from it except lest you should become angels or lest you should become immortal.' And he swore to them: 'Indeed, I am a sincere advisor to you.' Adam and Eve had never before witnessed anyone swearing by Allah falsely, so he beguiled them, and they ate from it trusting in his oath by Allah. That was from Adam before prophethood, and it was not a major sin (ذنب كبير) for which he deserved entry into the Fire; rather, it was among the forgiven minor sins (الصغائر الموهوبة) that are permissible for prophets before the descent of revelation to them. When Allah chose him and made him a prophet, he was infallible, committing neither a minor nor a major sin. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ** (And Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray. Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him clemently, and guided him), and the Mighty and Majestic said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ اصْطَفَىٰ آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرَانَ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ**," the *hadith*.

**I say:** Al-Saduq (may Allah have mercy on him) said after quoting the *hadith* at length: "The *hadith* is strange coming from the path of Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, given his being a *Nasibi* (نصبه) — *one who harbors hatred for the Ahl al-Bayt* — and his loathing and enmity toward the People of the House (عليه السلام)." End quote.

What is strange about it is only what he observed of its inclusion of the exoneration of the prophets, without looking deeply into the principles adopted therein. What he narrated of his (peace be upon him) answer regarding Adam does not agree with the school of the Imams of the People of the House, which is widely transmitted (المستفيض) from them, regarding the infallibility of the prophets from both minor and major sins, before prophethood and after it.

Furthermore, the answer involves a supposition in His saying, the Exalted: **مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَا** — "Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become...", similar to our saying: "Your Lord did not forbid you from this tree, He only forbade you from another, and He did not forbid you from another except lest you should become...", etc. Moreover, His saying, the Exalted: **مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَـٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ الْخَالِدِينَ** — "Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal" (7:20), and His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ يَا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ** — "He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of immortality, and a kingdom that never ages?’" (20:120), indicates that Iblis was indeed inciting them to eat from the very tree that was forbidden, by making them desire immortality and the kingdom that was withheld from them by the prohibition. Furthermore, the man—meaning Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm—had himself taken the correct and complete answer in the assembly of al-Ma'mun, as we narrated in the previous *hadith*. Thus, the narration is not without its issues, even if some of these points could potentially be refuted.

Al-Saduq narrated from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) from his fathers from Ali from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Adam and Eve's stay in the Garden until they were removed from it was only seven hours of the days of the world, until Allah sent them down on their same day."

In the *tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked while I was present: "How long did Adam and his wife stay in the Garden until their sin removed them from it?" He said: "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, breathed His spirit into Adam after the sun's decline on Friday, then He fashioned his wife from the lower part of his ribs, then He made His angels prostrate to him and settled him in His Garden on that same day. By Allah, he did not remain settled therein except for six hours of that same day until he disobeyed Allah, the Exalted. So Allah removed them both from it after sunset, and they were placed in the courtyard of the Garden until morning, when their nakedness became apparent to them and their Lord called out to them: 'Did I not forbid you from that tree?' Adam felt ashamed and humbled himself, saying: 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves and we confess our sins, so forgive us.' Allah said to them: 'Get down from My heavens to the earth, for no disobedient one shall neighbor Me in My Garden nor in My heavens.'"

**I say:** It is possible to derive what the narration contains regarding the manner of their exit—and that it was first from the Garden to its courtyard, and from its courtyard to the earth—from the repetition of the command to descend in the verse, given that it is a creative command (أمر تكويني) that is not subject to failure. Likewise, from the change in the context in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ** — "We said, ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in the paradise...’" until He said: **وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ** — "but do not approach this tree" (2:35), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَادَاهُمَا رَبُّكُمَا أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ** — "And their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree...?’" (7:22), where He expressed the first with "saying" (القول) and a near indication (الإشارة القريبة), and the second with "calling" (النداء) and a far indication (الإشارة البعيدة). However, the narration includes the creation of Eve from the lower part of Adam's ribs, as the Torah includes. The narrations from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) deem this false, as will come in the discussion regarding the creation of Adam, although it is possible to interpret her creation as being from the remainder of Adam's clay from the area near his ribs. As for the hours of his stay in the Garden, whether they were six or seven, the matter is minor, for it is only an approximation.

In *al-Kafi*: from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَتَلَقَّى آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ** — "Then Adam received certain words from his Lord" (2:37), he said: "[The words were:] 'There is no god but You, glory be to You, O Allah, and with Your praise; I have done evil and wronged myself, so forgive me, for You are the best of forgivers. There is no god but You, glory be to You, O Allah, and with Your praise; I have done evil and wronged myself, so have mercy on me, for You are the best of forgivers. There is no god but You, glory be to You, O Allah, and with Your praise; I have done evil and wronged myself, so have mercy on me, for You are the most merciful of the merciful. There is no god but You, glory be to You, O Allah, and with Your praise; I have done evil and wronged myself, so forgive me and turn to me clemently, for You are the All-clement, the All-merciful.'"

**I say:** Al-Saduq, al-Ayyashi, al-Qummi, and others have narrated this meaning, and something close to it has also been narrated through the paths of the Sunnis (أهل السنة والجماعة), and this may be derived from the apparent meaning of the verses of the story.

Al-Kulayni said in *al-Kafi*: In another narration regarding His saying: "Then Adam received certain words from his Lord," he said: "He asked Him by the right of Muhammad, Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, and al-Husayn."

**I say:** Al-Saduq, al-Ayyashi, al-Qummi, and others have also narrated this meaning. Something close to it has also been narrated through the paths of the Sunnis, as al-Suyuti narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "When Adam committed the sin that he committed, he raised his head to the heaven and said: 'I ask You by the right of Muhammad to forgive me.' So Allah revealed to him: 'And who is Muhammad?' He said: 'Blessed is Your Name; when You created me, I raised my head to Your Throne and found written upon it: "There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah." So I knew that there is no one with You of greater status than the one whose name You placed with Your Name.' So Allah revealed to him: 'O Adam, he is the last of the prophets from your progeny, and were it not for him, I would not have created you.'"

**I say:** Although this meaning is far from the apparent sense of the verses at first glance, deep reflection and contemplation upon them may bring it quite close. For His saying: "Then Adam received (تلقى)..." includes the meaning of taking along with welcoming, so it contains an indication of Adam taking these words from his Lord. Thus, it involves knowledge prior to the repentance. He (peace be upon him) had already learned all the names from his Lord, as the Exalted said to the angels: **إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفةً** — "‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth.’" They said: **أَتَجَعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ** — "‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’" He said: **إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ * وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا** — "‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’ And He taught Adam the Names, all of them" (2:30-31). This knowledge was of such a nature that it would inevitably remove every injustice and disobedience and be a cure for every disease; otherwise, the answer to what the angels raised would not be complete, nor would the proof be established against them. For He, the Glorified, did not mention anything in response to their saying: "who will cause corruption in it and shed blood," nor did He confront them with anything other than teaching Adam all the names. Thus, in it is the rectification of every corruption. You have already learned the reality of these names: that they are exalted beings hidden in the Unseen (غيب) of the heavens and the earth, and intermediaries for His, the Exalted, emanations to what is below them; no perfection is attained by any seeker of perfection except through their blessings. It has been reported in some reports that he saw the apparitions of the People of the House and their lights when he was taught the names; it has also been reported that he saw them when Allah brought forth his progeny from his back; and it has also been reported that he saw them while he was in the Garden; so refer back, and Allah is the Guide. Allah has left the matter of these words vague in His saying: **فَتَلَقَّى آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ** — "Then Adam received certain words from his Lord," by using the indefinite form (كلمات). And it has occurred in the Qur’an that the term "word" (الكلمة) is explicitly applied to an actual being in His saying: **بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** — "with a word from Him whose name is the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary" (3:45).

As for what some commentators have mentioned: that the words are those which Allah recounted from them both in Surah al-A'raf by His saying: **قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَالِدِينَ** — "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers’" (7:23), the response is: that the repentance (التوبة), as indicated by the verses in this Surah—meaning Surah al-Baqarah—occurred after the descent to the earth. The Exalted said: **وَقُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ** — "and We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another!’" until He said: **فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ** — "Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently" (2:36-37). These words were spoken by Adam and his wife before the descent while they were in the Garden, as in Surah al-A'raf. The Exalted said: **وَنَادَاهُمَا رَبُّكُمَا أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ** — "And their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree...?’" until He said: **قَالَا رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا** — "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves!’" until He said: **قَالَ اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ** — "He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another!’" (7:22-24). Rather, it is apparent that their saying: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves," was an act of self-abasement and humility from them in response to His, the Exalted, call, and an indication that the matter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, as He wills, after the confession that to Him belongs Lordship (الربوبية) and that they are wrongdoers on the brink of the danger of loss.

In the *tafsir* of al-Qummi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Moses asked his Lord to bring him and Adam together, so He brought them together. Moses said to him: 'O father, did Allah not create you with His hand, breathe into you of His spirit, make the angels prostrate to you, and command you not to eat from the tree? Why then did you disobey Him?' He said: 'O Moses, how long before my creation did you find my sin in the Torah?' He said: 'By thirty thousand years.' He said: 'Then it is that.'" Al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "So Adam defeated Moses in argument."

**I say:** Al-Suyuti narrated something close to this meaning in *al-Durr al-Manthur* through several paths from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

**In *al-Ilal*:** from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "By Allah, Allah created Adam for the world, and settled him in the Garden so that he might disobey Him and thus He would return him to that for which He created him."

**I say:** The narration of al-Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding a friend Adam had among the angels has already passed, and the *hadith* is in this meaning.

In *al-Ihtijaj*: in the argument of Ali with the Syrian when he asked him about the most noble valley on the face of the earth, he (peace be upon him) said: "A valley called Sarandib, where Adam fell from the heaven."

**I say:** This is contrasted by widely transmitted narrations indicating that he fell in the land of Mecca, and some of them have already passed. It is possible to reconcile them by the possibility of his first descending in Sarandib and then descending to the land of Mecca, and these are not two contradictory descents.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from al-Tabarani and Abu al-Shaykh in *al-Azamah* and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Dharr, who said: I said: "O Messenger of Allah, tell me, was Adam a prophet?" He said: "Yes, he was a prophet and a messenger; Allah spoke to him directly (قبلاً), saying to him: 'O Adam, dwell, you and your wife, in the Garden.'"

**I say:** The Sunnis have narrated something close to this meaning through several paths.
